rows.answer: No, the caption should not call the harmonic form 'the rational Hodge class'. A rational Hodge class refers to a cohomology class in H^{2p}(X, Q) that lies in the image of the cycle class map, whereas a harmonic form is a differential form that satisfies the Laplace equation. The harmonic representative of a rational Hodge class is a specific differential form, but the terms are not interchangeable. It would be more accurate to call it a 'harmonic representative of a rational Hodge class'.

The rational class alpha in H^4(X,Q) ∩ H^{2,2}(X) corresponds to a Hodge class, which can be represented by an algebraic cycle due to the Lefschetz theorem. The algebraic cycle is a linear combination of subvarieties of codimension 2. The Hodge conjecture, however, is still open for fourfolds, but in this specific case, the class alpha being (2,2) allows for an algebraic representative. This implies the existence of an algebraic cycle body representing alpha.

In this context, the codimension-k linear subspace P^{n-k} is the body, and its projection under a central projection is the shadow. The direction of projection is from the center of projection to the body, and it is theorem-defined by the theory of linear projections in algebraic geometry. Specifically, the direction is defined by the kernel of the projection map.

No, the first Hodge card should use H^0 and H^2 as the shadows, not H^1, as the Hodge decomposition for a smooth projective curve is given by H^1 = H^{1,0} ⊕ H^{0,1}, and H^1 is not the algebraic body being tested. Instead, H^0 and H^2 are used to compute the Euler characteristic. The Hodge diamond for a curve has H^0 in the top and H^2 in the bottom, with H^1 split into two parts.

rows.target_register: R3->R4 in the known p=1 case
rows.answer: The answer key can name the Néron-Severi group, as a rational (1,1) class is an element of this group. It must not generalize to the Chow group of cycles of arbitrary dimension, as the Néron-Severi group specifically concerns divisors, i.e., cycles of codimension 1. The Néron-Severi group is a subgroup of the Chow group of divisors.

The presentation of a rational (1,1) class as a curve class on a smooth projective surface S is indeed a known phenomenon, but it is not entirely new. This is related to the Hodge conjecture, which predicts that every Hodge class can be represented as a linear combination of cohomology classes of algebraic cycles. In the case of surfaces, the Hodge conjecture is known to hold for (1,1) classes, which can be represented by divisors or curves. However, the representation of a rational (1,1) class as a curve class can be considered a refinement of this phenomenon.

No, the card cannot silently upgrade this to an integral statement. The Hodge conjecture implies that a (2,2) Hodge class on a smooth projective threefold is algebraic, but it does not imply that a rational (2,2) class is represented by an integral curve class. The statement only guarantees the existence of a curve class representing the rational (2,2) class, not necessarily an integral one.

The Hodge decomposition on a compact Kähler manifold ensures that every de Rham cohomology class has a harmonic representative. For a rational (p,p) class, this harmonic form is also a rational linear combination of the harmonic forms representing the integral (p,p) classes. The generator can indeed reuse the smooth-projective Hodge structure without altering the source row, as the Hodge structure is preserved under harmonic representation. This follows from the properties of the Hodge decomposition and the fact that harmonic forms are closed and represent cohomology classes.

The locus where a fixed class stays of type (p,p) is indeed algebraic, as it can be defined by polynomial equations. This algebraicity is a consequence of the Global Torelli Theorem and the fact that the period map is algebraic. The algebraic cycle body, also known as the algebraic cycle space, is constructed as the union of such algebraic loci.

The answer key should clarify that the highlighted region represents the Poincaré dual of the (1,1) class, not the class itself. This is because a (1,1) class is a cohomology class, while the highlighted region appears to be a subvariety, which is more closely related to a homology class. The correct terminology would be to label the highlighted region as the Poincaré dual of the (1,1) class or as a representative of the dual homology class.
